coredata.dc:description: Photocatalytic fuel cells (PFCs) regarded as a potential sustainable technique, have been broadly reported. In this work, the carbon quantum dot-loaded TiO2 photoanode and sea urchin-like CuCoCe ternary metal oxide cathode materials are successfully synthesized and used to construct PFC systems for efficient tetracycline (TC) degradation (45 mg/L) and simultaneous electricity generation. The results demonstrate that the CQDs-modified TiO2 photoanode has improved absorption intensity in both the UV and visible regions, and the photocurrent density at 1.23 V vs RHE reached 1.31 mA cm−2, which is 1.3 times higher than that of the original TiO2 photoanode. The established PFC system achieves the highest removal ratio of 96.9 % for TC in 60 min with a maximum power density of 0.77 mW cm−2. The PFC system can operate efficiently over a wide pH range (3.0–9.0). Furthermore, quenching experiments and ESR spectra show that the main reactive oxygen species in the degradation process are •O2 −, 1O2 and •OH. This study provides meaningful way to develop multiple metal oxides as cathode of PFC system for efficient organic pollutant degradation and energy recovery.

head.ce:title: Three dimensional nickel foam carried sea urchin-like copper-cobalt-cerium cathode for enhanced tetracycline wastewater purification in photocatalytic fuel cell

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The CQDs-TiO2/BJS integrated photoanode and CuCoCe sea urchin-like cathode in the PFC system jointly activate the active oxygen to generate active oxygen species to degrade TC in wastewater.

Photocatalytic fuel cells (PFCs) regarded as a potential sustainable technique, have been broadly reported. In this work, the carbon quantum dot-loaded TiO2 photoanode and sea urchin-like CuCoCe ternary metal oxide cathode materials are successfully synthesized and used to construct PFC systems for efficient tetracycline (TC) degradation (45 mg/L) and simultaneous electricity generation. The results demonstrate that the CQDs-modified TiO2 photoanode has improved absorption intensity in both the UV and visible regions, and the photocurrent density at 1.23 V vs RHE reached 1.31 mA cm−2, which is 1.3 times higher than that of the original TiO2 photoanode. The established PFC system achieves the highest removal ratio of 96.9 % for TC in 60 min with a maximum power density of 0.77 mW cm−2. The PFC system can operate efficiently over a wide pH range (3.0–9.0). Furthermore, quenching experiments and ESR spectra show that the main reactive oxygen species in the degradation process are •O2 −, 1O2 and •OH. This study provides meaningful way to develop multiple metal oxides as cathode of PFC system for efficient organic pollutant degradation and energy recovery.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, an increasing number of antibiotics have been released into the water environment, leading to serious water pollution problems [1]. As a representative antibiotic, approximately 75 % of tetracycline (TC) is discharged into the sewage as a parent compound, because TC is not easily to be absorbed by the body, with 30 %–90 % finally excreted in feces and urine, respectively [2]. When undegraded TC eventually finds its way into natural water, it can cause potential harmful effects to ecosystems and the human body [3]. Due to the difficult degradation properties of tetracycline itself, its discharge into the aqueous environment can cause great harm [4]. Therefore, a suitable degradation technology is urgently needed to remove the residual TC from sewage.

In recent decades, photoelectrochemical (PEC) technology has shown great potential in the elimination of organics [5–7]. Photocatalytic fuel cells (PFCs), developed from PECs, are a potential prospective technology for organic pollutant degradation and power generation [8,9]. The advantage of PFC is that it can achieve synchronous power generation and organic pollutant removal [10,11]. In this system, organic compounds can be oxidized at the photoanode to release electrons, which will be transferred to the reverse cathode to participate in the production of reactive oxygen species and generate electrical energy. The common used photoanode materials in PFC systems are WO3, TiO2, BiVO4, g-C3N4, and so on [12–15]. Among them, TiO2 has attracted extensive research because of its high stability, corrosion resistance, and nontoxicity for potential applications [16–19]. However, it cannot be activated by visible light because of its rather large band gap [18]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), an eco-friendly material with low cost, have been shown to have a broad absorption across the solar spectrum [20,21]. The loading of CQDs can significantly promote the absorption performance of TiO2 in the visible region [22], thus greatly improving the catalytic performance of the materials. Meanwhile, the choice of cathode materials has also received attention form many researchers [23–25]. Xiaoyu Zhong et al. constructed a PEC system with a Ag/N-doped TiO2 photoanode and a P-Pd cathode, which enhanced the generation of hydroxyl radicals on the cathode surface and effectively removed TC from wastewater [26]. Wenbo Wu et al. improved the ability of the nonradical pathway to degrade TC by introducing N and S elements into magnetic biochar to modulate the active electrons [27]. Tayebeh Parvizi et al. prepared Ppy/MWCNT/Fe3O4 materials as cathodes for PFC systems, which can increase the yield of hydrogen peroxide and thus produce reactive species to promote the degradation of pollutants [28]. Su et al. prepared Fe-NiCo2S4 nickel foam cathodes and the doping of iron promoted the redox cycling and stability of the cathodes [7].

CuO can enhance the selectivity of the 2-electron reduction process of oxygen and provide the cathode with more electron-rich centers, which can promote the redox cycle of other metal ions and the rapid conversion of H2O2 to hydroxyl radicals (•OH) [29,30]. The Co2+/Co3+ pair possesses a steady redox potential and demonstrates excellent properties in the H2O2-mediated degradation process of organic pollutants [31,32]. The symbiotic coexistence of Cu-Co binary metals induced interfacial effects, which fully promoted the catalytic reaction. However, the CuCo binary metal has the shortcomings of a high reaction temperature, the easy formation of the spinel phase, and the reduction of the active site [31]. These problems can be mitigated by introducing additives to improve the catalytic activity of the CuCo species. Oxygen vacancies (Vo) can be easily formed on the surface of CeO2 by the reduction process of Ce4+ to Ce3+, which can release free electrons or promote oxygen activation [33]. Cerium-based composite catalysts have been widely used for the oxidation of carbon monoxide, methane [34], propylene [35], and volatile organic compounds [36–38], etc. There are few studies on the photoelecatalysis of organic pollutants. Therefore, we chose to introduce CeO2 into CuCo bimetallic oxide as the cathode material for this system.

In this paper, CuCoCe was selected as the cathode and utilized to build a PFC system with a CQDs-TiO2 photoanode. CuCoCe ternary metal oxides were synthesized using a facile hydrothermal method and were fully characterized. The effects of solution pH, aeration method, and illumination on the degradation of TC were investigated in this system. The mechanism of the degradation process was also proposed. In this study, we synthesized a 3D sea urchin-shaped CuCoCe ternary metal oxide as the cathode material and CQDs-TiO2 as the photoanode to construct a novel PFC system for degrading TC while producing electricity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs-TiO2/BJS (buried junction silicon cell) photoanode
ce:section.ce:para: The chemical reagents used in this experiment are shown in Table S1. TiO2 photoanodes were synthesized using our previously reported method [39]. First, a TiO2 crystal seed layer was deposited on the pretreated FTO glass (3 cm × 3 cm). Typically, tetra butyl titanate (TBT, 0.1 mL) was dropped directly into 10 mL of anhydrous solution under agitation for half an hour to mix it completely. The above solution was then spin-coated onto the FTO according to the procedure of 2500 rpm for 20 s, followed by baking on a heating plate at 60 °C for half an hour. Finally, the prepared film on FTO was annealed at 450 °C for 2 h to obtain the ultimate TiO2 seed layer.

The TiO2 photoanode was prepared using an ordinary hydrothermal method. TBT (0.72 mL) was first dropped into a 60 mL solution consisting of an equal volume of deionized water (DI) and hydrochloric acid, followed by keep stirring until clarification. The above solution was then shifted to an autoclave with a 100 mL volume. Afterward, the TiO2 seed layer film was completely immersed in the solution and hydrothermally heated at 170 °C for 6 h. The obtained film cooled down to room temperature was finally rinsed with water and baked on a hot plate.

CQDs are synthesized by a previously reported one-step alkali-assisted sonochemical approach [40]. Normally, NaOH solution (1 M, 50 mL) was added and sonicated for 6 h after dissolving glucose (9 g) in DI (50 mL). The pH of the above solution was regulated to neutral with hydrochloric acid, and then 100 mL of ethanol was added. The above mixed solution was stirred for one hour. In the preparation process of CQDs-TiO2 photoanodes, the above solution (0.5 mL) was obtained and dip-coated onto the TiO2 film using a transfer liquid gun, and then the coating was spun at 1000 rpm for 10 s, followed by baking on a heating plate at 100 °C for half an hour. The above spin-coating process was repeated twice and CQDs-TiO2 photoanodes were then successfully prepared. Afterward, the prepared CQDs-TiO2 photoanode is connected to a BJS with a rated voltage of 2 V and a nominal current of 35 mA through copper wires using conductive Ag adhesive to form a CQDs-TiO2/BJS composite photoanode.

Nickel foam (NF) with dimensions of 50 × 25 × 3 mm3 was used as the supporting substrate. Before use, the surface oxide layer of the NF was removed first by diluted H2SO4 for half an hour and then dried in a vacuum oven [39]. CuCoCe electrodes were prepared according to the following steps: 2 mmol Co(NO3)3·6H2O, 2 mmol Cu(NO3)3·3H2O, 20 mmol of urea and 2 mmol Ce(NO3)3·6H2O were dissolved in 50 mL of DI water and completely blended for half an hour. The above mixture was then shifted to an autoclave with the pretreated nickel foam completely immersed into the solution, followed by a hydrothermal process at 140 °C for 6 h. The resulting electrodes were washed with ethanol and DI water, and then naturally dried, followed by annealing in a furnace at 350 °C for 2 h.
The physicochemical properties of the electrode were characterized by performing XRD, TEM, SEM, EDS, XPS, and BET tests. The specific test methods are described in the supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoelectrochemical (PEC) tests and PFC system construction
ce:section.ce:para: The PEC properties of the obtained photoanode were determined on an AUTOLAB through a three-electrode system including the CQDs-TiO2/BJS photo-anode (working electrode), Pt plate (auxiliary electrode), and Ag/AgCl (compared electrode). Chopped photocurrent density-time (J-t) and photocurrent-potential (J-V) curves were obtained by testing in a solution containing 0.1 M sodium sulfate under simulated light irradiation.

A two-electrode system consisting of a CQDs-TiO2/BJS photoanode and a CuCoCe cathode was subjected to degradation experiments in a quartz cell (4.5 × 4 × 4 cm3) without load resistance and external bias. The electrolyte solution was composed of the model contaminant tetracycline (30 mL, 45 mg/L) and 0.1 M sodium sulfate. A 300 W Xe lamp (Beijing Perfect Light Co., Ltd., China) was used to supply a simulated light source to the system with an irradiation intensity of 100 mW cm−2. During degradation, the absorbance of the solution was tested at 357 nm using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-2600). The TOC content in the degradation solution was tested using a TOC analyzer (Shimadzu TOC-L). The degradation experiments carried out under different conditions were repeated three times during the course of the experiment to check the accuracy. The final graph shows the mean values with error bars.

Specific quenching agents were added to conduct free radical quenching experiments before the degradation experiments started. First, the optimal addition amounts of quenching reagents were determined. In the PFC system with CuCoCe as the cathode, the additive amounts of p-phenylquinone (PBQ), furfuryl alcohol (FFA), EDTA-2Na, and tert-butyl alcohol (TBA) were 1.0 mL, 25 μL, 1.0 mL, and 0.7 mL, respectively.

Electrocatalytic (EC) degradation: The CQDs-TiO2/BJS photoanode was replaced with a Pt sheet, and a positive bias voltage equal to the voltage of the PFC system was imposed on the cathode using an electrochemical workstation for the removal experiments of TC. The I-V characteristics of the CoCuCe PFC system were acquired under continuous aeration by scanning within the voltage range of −2.3 V and 0 V at a speed of 20 mV s−1.

As shown in Fig. 1 a, CQDs-TiO2 were analyzed using TEM images, and it can be found that CQDs were indeed deposited on the TiO2 nanorods. HRTEM images of CQDs-TiO2 are shown in Fig. 1b, the (110) plane spacing of rutile-phase TiO2 is 0.324 nm, and the lattice spacings of 0.21 nm and 0.32 nm correspond to the (100) and (002) crystal planes of CQDs graphite, respectively [13,21,41]. The crystal pattern of CQDs-TiO2 was represented and then compared with that of TiO2 (Fig. S1) in order to better demonstrate the synthesis of CQDs-TiO2 photoanodes, and it can be found that the XRD spectrum of the CQDs-TiO2 photoanode has three significant peaks at 36.1°, 41.2° and 62.7°, matching rutile (PDF#03-1122), and the rest of the peaks appearing at 26.1°, 37.3°, 51.6°, 55.8°, 65.1°, and 69.5° belong to SnO2 (PDF#46-1088), which indicates that the signal of CQDs cannot be detected by XRD [11,13]. Fig. S2 shows the vertical view and cross-sectional SEM images of the prepared TiO2 and CQDs-TiO2 photoanodes, and it can be seen that the TiO2 nanorods are grown vertically and closely on the FTO with an average thickness of 2.6 µm. The spectral absorption characteristics of TiO2 and CQDs-TiO2 photoanodes were tested as shown in Fig. S3. The absorption edge wavelength of TiO2 photoanode was 405 nm, and the absorption edge wavelength of CQDs-TiO2 photoanode was redshifted to 422 nm after loading CQDs, and its absorption intensity was higher than that of the TiO2 photoanode in both the UV region and the visible region. At the same time, a silicon solar panel was attached to the back of CQDs-TiO2 photoanode, and it can be seen from Fig. S3 that the added silicon solar panel greatly expands the light absorption and improves the utilization of natural light.

It was found from the J-V curves of TiO2 and CQDs-TiO2 photoanodes (Fig. 1c) that the TiO2 photoanode demonstrated a photocurrent density of 1.0 mA cm−2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE. After loading CQDs, the value reaches 1.31 mA cm−2, which is 1.3 times as high as that of the original TiO2, indicating an improvement in the photocurrent property of CQDs-TiO2. Moreover, it can be seen in Fig. 1d that the photocurrent density of the CQDs-TiO2/BJS photoanode is 0.97 mA cm−2, which indicates that the system can maintain long-term stability without any external bias.

As shown in Fig. 2 a, nickel foam has a 3D skeleton structure, and Fig. 2b and c further shows that the surface of the nickel foam skeleton is smooth. As can be seen in Fig. 1d, the nickel foam still maintains its three-dimensional skeleton structure after loading with a CuCo catalyst. The three-dimensional hollow skeleton construction ensures the unrestricted flow of products and reactants [42]. From Fig. 2 e, f, it can be seen that the surface of nickel foam becomes rough and uneven after loading the CuCo catalyst. The CuCo sea urchin-like nanospheres are distributed uniformly on the nickel foam skeleton and consist of tiny nanoneedles. Afterward, when Ce elements were further introduced, a denser and more uniform layer of material grew on the skeleton surface of NF (Fig. 2 g, h, j), and the sea urchin-like spheres were more pronounced and expanded in volume under the same 5 µm image, as shown in Fig. 2h. The special three-dimensional nanoneedle structure enabled the material to possess greater specific surface area, which may supply more active sites for catalytic reactions and be beneficial to the catalytic efficiency. The occurred morphological change facilitates the adsorption and transfer of active substances on the catalyst, thus promoting the degradation of tetracycline substances [43]. The reason for the formation of this morphology is that when nickel foam is immersed into the precursor solution containing Cu ions, Co ions, and Ce ions, the surface will adsorb these metal ions to form a seed layer first, and with the gradual increase in reaction temperature in the hydrothermal process, the urea in the precursor solution starts to hydrolyze at 70 °C and the hydroxide ions are released to react with the metal ions in a uniform precipitation. According to Banfield's theory of directional attachment, the crystals start from the seed layer and radiate outward to grow a structure of nanoneedles. Finally, the three-dimensional sea urchin-like CuCoCe oxide metal material is obtained by high-temperature calcination in a resistance furnace.

Fig. S4 demonstrates that the Cu, Co, and Ce elements are all distributed uniformly within the CuCoCe catalyst in the selected mapping region. The surface elements of the sample are shown by EDS (Fig. S5). The results demonstrated that the atomic percentages of Cu, Co, and Ce were 18.12 %, 81.47 %, and 0.41 %, respectively, which proved the successful synthesis of the CuCoCe catalyst. In addition, a mismatch between the atomic percentages and the formula can be found, which may be due to approximate characteristic X-ray intensities of Ni(substrate of the cathode) and Co.

The N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm of CuCoCe is exhibited in Fig. S6. The CuCoCe cathode exhibited a representative type IV nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherm, indicating presence of mesopores. The details of the surface properties of CuCo and CuCoCe are shown in Table S2. It can be seen from the table that the surface area of CuCo is 4.24 m2/g and the pore volume is 0.01 cm3/g. After further introduction of Ce into CuCo, the specific surface area and pore volume of CuCoCe increased to 9.22 m2/g and 0.02 cm3/g, respectively. The great specific surface area and mesoporous structure contribute to the catalytic effect, allowing for adsorption of more reactants and contaminants, providing sufficient active sites for catalytic reactions, and providing multiple channels for efficient electron transfer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Organic degradation and power production property characterization

ce:section.ce:para: First, various PFC systems with diverse cathodes were constructed using CQDs-TiO2/BJS as the photoanode, and 45 mg/L TC was adopted as the model pollutant in all the devices operated with no external bias applied, complete aeration, and AM 1.5 G irradiation. The system was continuously aerated and the oxygen concentration remained near 8.70 mg/L throughout the degradation process. Before the degradation process started, the assembled PFC systems were placed in an environment sheltered from any light for half an hour and their adsorption ratios were measured to be approximately 5 %, which has almost no effect on the experiments.

TC degradation curves in PFC systems with various cathodes are shown in Fig. 5 (a), and it can be seen that the removal efficiency of TC in the system is approximately 48.6 % within 60 min when pristine nickel foam is used as the cathode. However, when the CuCo catalyst is loaded on nickel foam, its removal ratio for TC can reach 77.2 %. After the introduction of the Ce into the material, the removal ratio of the CuCoCe PFC system for TC is up to 96.6 %. To investigate the effect of BJS on degradation property of PFC system, TC degradation in PFC system consisting of CQDs-TiO2 photoanode and CuCoCe cathode under the same conditions was conducted and the removal effect is shown in Fig. S7. It can be seen that in the absence of BJS, the removal ratio is 78.44 % in 60 min. The use of a self-biased composite photoanode consisting of a CQDs-TiO2 photoanode film and BJS can fully utilize solar energy in the entire spectral range and improve the photogenerated carrier utilization, which in turn improves its degradation efficiency. In addition, the PFC system constructed with CQDs-TiO2/BJS photoanode and CuCoCe cathode shows an improvement in degradation and power generation compared with other reported PFC systems that also contain BJS (Table S3).

To confirm the importance of O2 during the degradation process, removal ratios of CuCoCe PFC systems under diverse aeration conditions were conducted and compared (Fig. S8). The removal efficiency of TC was as high as 96.6 % in 60 min when air was aerated into the system. However, the removal ratio of the system decreased rapidly to 45.50 % when no air was aerated into the system. To ensure that there was no possibility of dissolved oxygen in the system, nitrogen was aerated into the system and the removal efficiency was further reduced, dropping to 33.40 % within 60 min. From the above phenomenon, it can be found that O2 has an extremely important role in TC degradation. To reveal the role of CuCoCe in the constructed system, CuCoCe cathodes were fixed in the electrocatalytic (EC) system and operated under the same conditions as the PFC system to explore the TC degradation efficiencies as exhibited in Fig. 5(b). A removal efficiency of 46.5 % for TC in the absence of light can be seen in the EC system, demonstrating that CuCoCe cathode played an important part in the constructed system.

To investigate the mineralization ability of TC in the CuCoCe PFC system, an experiment on the TOC removal ratio was conducted on the system as exhibited in Fig. S9. The results demonstrated that the TOC removal ratio could reach 75.0 % after 60 min of operation, which indicated that the CuCoCe PFC system possesses excellent mineralization ability.

It has been documented that pH can influence the degradation of organic pollutants by various PFC systems [49,50]. As shown in Fig. 5(c), the CuCoCe PFC system degraded TC very well under both acidic and neutral pH conditions. When the pH values were 3, 5 and 9, the removal ratios of TC were all above 90 %, reaching 91.5 %, 91.6 %, and 90.5 %, respectively. However, when the pH value was 11, the system achieved a minimum removal ratio of 85.3 %. However, under alkaline conditions, a higher number of hydroxide ions affects the generation of H2O2 and also adversely affects the decomposition of H2O2 by Cu, Co and Ce ions [51–53], which further affects the generation of reactive substances and is therefore unfavorable for the degradation of TC under alkaline conditions.

To confirm the stability of the CuCoCe cathode, the TC was subjected to the same operating conditions for five cycles. It was noteworthy that removal ratios of TC in the five experiments were 96.9 %, 95.34 %, 94.71 %, 93.54 %, and 93.30 %, respectively (Fig. 5d). indicating that the CuCoCe PFC system is suitable for long-term recycling applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Power production capability of the CuCoCe PFC system
ce:section.ce:para:
The power generation capability in the CuCoCe PFC system was investigated by conducting photocurrent-voltage and power density tests. The following equation was adopted to determine the power density:
p
(
mW cm
- 2
)
= J
(
mA cm
- 2
)
+
|
V bias
( V )
|
in which J and V bias correspond to the photocurrent density and bias applied to the photoanode with respect to the cathode, respectively.

The system established by the CuCoCe cathode and CQDs-TiO2/BJS photoanode was studied for the power production capability. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the maximum power density value of this system was 0.77 mW cm−2 with a short circuit current of 0.78 mA cm−2 and an open circuit voltage of 2.51 V, and the optimum power generation capacity was obtained. As shown in Fig. S10, pure NF, CuCo, and CuCoCe PFC systems were compared to verify the outstanding power production property of the CuCoCe PFC system. The Pmax value are 0.24 and 0.68 mW cm−2 for NF and CuCo PFC systems, respectively. The results showed that CuCoCe PFC system obtained the optimum power generation performance. Fig. 6b shows the photocurrent-time image of the CuCoCe PFC system, and it is not difficult to find that the output current density is always 0.78 × 10−3 A cm−2, which confirms that the stability of the studied PFC system is excellent throughout the degradation process. Fig. S11 shows that the photocurrent density in the CuCoCe PFC system containing TC and Na2SO4 is slightly higher than that including only Na2SO4 as the electrolyte. This phenomenon indicates that the increased theoretical power generated in the organic degradation process was greater than the power flowing through the external circuit, which means that the electrons from TC degradation were only partly transferred to the cathode through the external circuit.

To detect the major reactive oxygen species (ROS) responsible for the degradation of TC in the CuCoCe PFC system, a free radical quenching experiment and an ESR test were performed, respectively. Here, TBA was adopted as •OH trapping agent [54]. As shown in Fig. S12, the removal efficiency of the CuCoCe PFC system was 96.6 % when no quenching agents were added. The removal ratio was reduced by 11.6 % when TBA was added. PBQ is a general •O2 − inhibitor capable of reducing oxygen production and free radical formation by trapping electrons [55]. In the presence of PBQ and FFA, the removal efficiency of TC was reduced from 96.9 % to 50 % and 71 %, respectively. It can be concluded that •O2 − and 1O2 play an important role in the degradation of the CuCoCe PFC system. In addition, EDTA-2Na was used as a scavenger of holes (h+), and by comparison, we found that a lesser role was played by holes in TC degradation. Therefore, it can be inferred that •O2 −, 1O2, and •OH are the main ROS in the CuCoCe PFC system.

XPS spectra of CuCoCe before and after the degradation process were tested to further analyze the catalytic mechanism in the CuCoCe PFC system, as shown in Fig. 7 . Fig. 7a exhibits the Co 2p XPS spectra of the synthesized CuCoCe cathode. Two main peaks ascribed to Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2 appeared at 780.7 eV and 795.9 eV, respectively. The peak located at 785.6 eV is the satellite peak. The Co 2p3/2 peaks located at 779.7 eV and 781.3 eV can be decomposed into two peaks associated with Co3+ and Co2+, respectively. Moreover, the two peaks situated at 794.8 eV and 796.3 eV should be ascribed to Co3+ and Co2+ [56]. The relative amount of Co3+ increased from 37.7 % to 41.6 % and the relative amount of Co2+ reduced from 62.3 % to 58.9 % after the reaction. It could be attributed to the truth that Co2+ provides the electrons to decompose H2O2 (Eq. (1)) [57]. To investigate the ability of Cu, Co, and Ce ions to decompose H2O2 and produce reactive species in PFC systems, degradation and H2O2 production experiments were performed for different combinations of metal ions. From the results shown in Fig. S13, the ranking orders of H2O2 concentration and TC removal efficiency in various systems are just opposite. CuCe PFC system achieved the highest H2O2 concentration and the lowest TC removal ratio, which can be ascribed to the fact that CuO can promote the selective process of two-electron reduction of oxygen and the weak ability of Cu and Ce to decompose H2O2 for the formation of other ROS. Secondly, the CuCo PFC system produced the second highest amount of H2O2, which may be due to the fact that Co ions have a greater ability to decompose H2O2 than Ce ions. The ability of the CoCe PFC system to catalyze the generation of H2O2 is weaker compared to the above two systems, probably because the generated H2O2 is activated by Co and Ce species to produce •OH and its ability to catalyze the generation of H2O2 becomes correspondingly weaker due to the lack of Cu element. Although the H2O2 content produced in this system was reduced, its degradation effect was instead stronger than that of the two PFC systems (CuCe and CuCo), as shown in Fig. S13a, which was attributed to the fact that the high oxygen activation capacity of Co-based oxides was conducive to the generation of superoxide radicals and singlet oxygen, and the cycling process of Ce ions was beneficial to the formation of oxygen vacancies, which further facilitated the activation of oxygen and the generation of reactive oxygen species. Based on the above analysis, it is not difficult to understand that the CuCoCe PFC system can achieve a higher TC removal ratio with low H2O2 production. Meanwhile, Co2+ can be regenerated by reactions (3) and (4) to maintain the long-term steady operation of the system [45].
(1) Co2+ + H2O2 → Co3++OH− + •OH
(2) 2H+ + O2 + 2e− → H2O2
(3) Co3+ + •O2 − → 1O2 + Co2+
(4) Cu+ + Co3+ → Cu2+ + Co2+

The copper ions in the fine spectral XPS analysis are demonstrated in Fig. 7b. As can be seen from the figure that the substance has Cu+, Cu2+, and a satellite peak. The peaks corresponding to Cu+ are situated at 932.7 eV and 952.5 eV, while the peaks at 934.1 eV and 954.1 eV are attributed to Cu2+. In addition, three satellite peaks at 941.1 eV, 943.7 eV, and 962.6 eV can be found in the spectrum, which proves the presence of CuO [58]. According to the quantitative analysis, the relative amounts of Cu+ and Cu2+ in the pristine cathode were 48.7 % and 51.3 %. After use, the content of Cu+ increased to 57.7 % and the content of Cu2+ decreased to 42.3 %. The decrease in the relative Cu2+ content indicates that H2O2 might be reduced by Cu2+ to produce hydroxyl radicals (Eq. (5)) [59]. Moreover, oxygen might be reduced by Cu+ to produce superoxide radicals (Eq. (6)). Superoxide radicals produced by Cu+-excited dissolved oxygen can also form H2O2 (Eq. (6) and (7)) [60]. Due to the stable operation of the system, there might be a reaction between Cu+ and Ce4+ allowing the CuCoCe cathode to return to its initial state (Eq. (8)).
(5) Cu2+ + H2O2 → Cu+ + •OH + H+
(6) Cu+ + O2 → Cu2+ + •O2 −
(7) •O2 − + e− + 2H+ → H2O2
(8) Cu+ + Ce4+ → Cu2+ + Ce3+

Fig. 7c demonstrates the spectrum of Ce ions. The Ce 3d peak was decomposed into eight peaks attributed to Ce3+ and Ce4+. The two peaks at 878.9 and 903.4 eV, labeled as u1 and v1, can be ascribed to the low state of Ce3+ species, and six peaks at 821.8, 889.1, 898.2, 901.2, 908.5 and 917.3 eV, marked u, u2, u3, v, v2, and v3, correspond to the characteristic peaks of Ce4+ at Ce 3d5/2 and Ce 3d3/2 [48,61]. The increased amount of Ce3+ species implies that the CuCoCe composite catalyst after the reaction contains more oxygen vacancies. Meanwhile, cerium ions can also activate oxygen and hydrogen peroxide, producing •O2 − and •OH (Eq. (9) and Eq. (10)) [35]. There may be a synergistic effect between Co ions, Cu ions, and Ce ions (Eq. (8) and (11)), making the cathode material maintain its pristine state.
(9) Ce3++O2 → •O2 − + Ce4+
(10) Ce3+ + H2O2 → Ce4+ + OH− + •OH
(11) Co2+ + Ce4+ → Co3+ + Ce3+

Fig. 7d depicts the O 1s peaks of CuCoCe before and after the degradation process. In this system, the metal ions consume oxygen to generate ROS and oxygen itself can obtain electrons to form superoxide radicals (Equation (12)). The peak at 529.5 eV can be attributed to lattice oxygen (Olatt) belonging to the CeO2, CoO, and CuO phases. After being used for TC degradation, the relative amount of Olatt dropped from 51.1 % to 40.1 %, which indicated that the lattice oxygen was consumed by oxidation (Equation (13)). Another peak situated at 531.3 eV corresponds to the oxygen vacancy (Ov) [62]. After the degradation process, the relative amount of Ov increased from 30.6 % to 34.3 %, which was also in agreement with the increase in the relative content of Ce3+, due to the generation of Ce3+ associated with the formation of Ov in CeO2 (Equation (14)). The peak at 532.6 eV can be ascribed to adsorbed molecular oxygen (Oads), including O2−, O2 −, or O− and OH−, and its relative content increases from 18.3 % to 25.6 %, demonstrating that Olatt was oxidized in the degradation process and that Ov could also react and transform to 1O2(Equation (15)) [63].
(12) O2 + e− → •O2 −
(13) Olatt → 1O2
(14) Ce4+ → Ce3+ + Ov
(15) Ov → 1O2

Since the cathode of the PFC system is a multimetal oxide, the leaching of metal ions needs to be taken into account. Therefore, we used atomic spectrophotometry to measure the concentrations of four metal ions including Cu, Co, Ce, and Ni in the system after the degradation was completed. The test results show that the concentrations of total Ni ion, Co ion, and Cu ion in the solution were 0.287 mg/L, 0.567 mg/L and 0.213 mg/L, respectively, and Ce ion were not checked in the model water. Based on the above data, conclusion can be gained that the operation of CuCoCe PFC system will not lead to any additional pollution to the aquatic environment.

ESR test can be performed by using trapping agents to capture the different reactive substances in the degradation process [48]. The chara-cteristic signal of •OH can be observed in Fig. 8 a, providing proof for the existence of hydroxyl radicals in the CuCoCe PFC system, which can be attributed to the decomposition of H2O2 (Eq. (1), (5), and (10)). Super-oxide radicals (•O2 −) are produced by the single electron reduction of oxygen (equation (12)). Meanwhile, in the CuCoCe PFC system, Co and Ce ions can act as electron shuttling agents to accelerate the decomposition of H2O2 and generate •O2 − (Eq. (1) and (12)). According to the above analysis, Co2+/Co3+ and Ce3+/Ce4+ can generate superoxide radicals during decomposition; thus, the characteristic signal of DMPO-•O2 − can be recognized.

The typical ESR spectroscopy were acquired using TEMP as spin trapping compounds, demonstrating the presence of 1O2 (Fig. 8b). In the CuCoCe system, a single linear state of oxygen (1O2) can be produced due to the intense oxidation of Co3+ and the formation of metal oxide lattice oxygen and oxygen vacancies (Eq. (3), (13) and (15)).

In this paper, a ternary metal oxide CuCoCe loaded on nickel foam as cathode material was first prepared to construct a new PFC system for the effective degradation of TC in wastewater within a wide pH range. The introduction of the element Ce led to the formation of a special spherical nano-needle structure of copper-cobalt-cerium metal oxides, which facilitates the formation of oxygen vacancies on the cathode and thus promotes the production of reactive oxygen species. The established CuCoCe PFC system showed a TC removal ratio of 96.9 % and TOC degradation ratio of 75 % within 60 min. After five runs, there was no significant decrease in the removal ratio of the system. Compared to most existing PFC systems, this system has an improvement in degradation time and degradation effect [39,64]. It is hoped that this work will provide novel ideas for the design of efficient and stable PFC systems using polymetal oxide cathode.
